request.question: Susan May Pratt played the part of Mandella in a movie that came out in which year?

title: 10 Things I Hate About You
Susan May Pratt
Lenora Mandella
Jerome Napoleon Bonaparte II
Black-eyed Susan
Open Water 2: Adrift
Susan May Williams
Imogen Boorman
Andy Pratt (baseball)
Kyla Pratt
sentences: 10 Things I Hate About You is a 1999 American romantic comedy film directed by Gil Junger and starring Julia Stiles, Heath Ledger, Joseph Gordon-Levitt, and Larisa Oleynik.

The screenplay, written by Karen McCullah Lutz and Kirsten Smith, is a modernization of William Shakespeare's late-16th century comedy "The Taming of the Shrew", retold in a late-1990s American high school setting.

In the story, new student Cameron (Gordon-Levitt) is smitten with Bianca (Oleynik) and, in order to get around her father's strict rules on dating, attempts to get bad boy Patrick (Ledger) to date Bianca's ill-tempered sister, Kat (Stiles).
The film is titled after a poem written by Kat about her bittersweet romance with Patrick.
Much of the filming took place in the Seattle metropolitan area, with many scenes shot at Stadium High School in Tacoma.
Susan May Pratt (born February 8, 1974) is an American actress.
She played Mandella in "10 Things I Hate About You", Alicia in "Drive Me Crazy", and Maureen Cummings in "Center Stage".
Lenora Mandella (4 May 1931 - 12 August 2005) played shortstop and pitcher in the All-American Girls Professional Baseball League between 1949 and 1951.
She both batted and threw right-handed.
Her nickname was Smokey.
She measured in at 5 feet 7 inches and weighed 145 pounds.
Jerome-Napoleon Bonaparte II (1830-1893) was an American soldier who served in the French Army.
He was the son of Jérôme Napoléon Bonaparte and Susan May Williams.
Black-eyed Susan may refer to one of several things:

Open Water 2: Adrift (called Adrift in the UK, Australia, and various other countries, and called Open Water 2 in Germany) is a 2006 psychological horror film filmed entirely in Malta, starring Eric Dane, Susan May Pratt, Richard Speight, Jr., Niklaus Lange, Ali Hillis, and Cameron Richardson.
Promotional posters claim the film is based on actual events.

Susan May Williams Bonaparte (April 2, 1812 – September 15, 1881) was the daughter of Benjamin Williams, a prominent Baltimore merchant (originally from Roxbury, Massachusetts), and his wife, Sarah Copeland, widow of Nathaniel Morton.

In response to the opening of the Erie Canal, which was in direct competition with the port of Baltimore, her father became one of the founders of the Baltimore and Ohio Railroad, the first railroad company in the United States, chartered on April 24, 1827.
Imogen May Pratt Boorman (born 13 May 1971) is an English film actress and television actress.
She is known for portraying Tiffany in the horror film "", Lorina in "Dreamchild", Clothhide in "May to December" and Hannah Preston in "Westbeach".
Andrew Elias Pratt (born August 27, 1979) is a former Major League Baseball pitcher who played for the Atlanta Braves and Chicago Cubs.
Pratt was selected by the Texas Rangers in the 9th round of the 1998 Major League Baseball Draft and had a career ERA of 15.00 in 5 career appearances with the Braves and Cubs.
He also played in the Texas Rangers organization from - and Milwaukee Brewers organization from -.
After his release from the Brewers organization in 2006, Pratt played the rest of the season for the Somerset Patriots of the independent Atlantic League.
He has not played professionally since.
Kyla Alissa Pratt is an American actress.

In television, she is known for her roles in the popular U.S. children's television series "Barney & Friends" and the Disney Channel series "The Proud Family" (which lasted for 3 seasons) and "One on One" (which lasted for 5 seasons).

After playing the daughter of Eddie Murphy's character in the films "Dr. Dolittle" and "Dr. Dolittle 2", Pratt became the main character in the remake series of the franchise such as "Dr. Dolittle 3", "", and "".
Pratt has also been in the films "Fat Albert", "Hotel for Dogs", and "The Proud Family Movie".
She has also played in the series "Let's Stay Together".

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Produce a compact, structured first-hop summary that helps the next agent fetch the missing second-hop facts. The summary must be one labeled block with these four short sections (each 1–4 lines):

1) Evidence found — For each concrete target term or name mentioned in the passages, list the target and which passage(s) mention it, and quote a short verbatim excerpt (≤15 words) or any exact numeric/date token from the passage. Preserve the quoted text and any numeric/date formatting exactly as it appears in the passages. Include passage identifier(s) exactly as given (title or index).

2) Ambiguities — Enumerate any names or terms that correspond to multiple candidate pages or where the passages return multiple variants or conflicting values. For each ambiguous item, list the candidate labels or note the conflict using only tokens from the passages where possible.

3) Bridge / missing fact — State exactly which relation(s) or fact(s) needed to answer the question are not present or are unresolved in the provided passages (e.g., birth date, publication year, author of adaptation, founding year, award count). Be explicit and concise.

4) Retrieval direction — Provide 2–4 concrete next-hop retrievals: specific page titles or disambiguation pages, or precise search queries the next agent should run to fetch the missing bridge facts. Use actionable, precise phrasing (page title or quoted query). End this section with an explicit deferral of the final answer when the passages do not unambiguously supply all required facts.

Formatting and behavior rules:
- Produce only the single field `summary` as the output. The `summary` text must follow the four labeled sections above.
- Always quote verbatim evidence (names, short excerpts, dates, numbers) exactly as in the passages; do not reformat numeric/date tokens.
- Do NOT produce a final answer here unless every required fact to resolve the question is present and unambiguous in the provided passages (i.e., each named entity corresponds to a single candidate and the needed relation value is explicitly present). If facts are unambiguous and complete, you may state that and include a concise final conclusion in the `summary`; otherwise explicitly defer (for example: "Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets below.").
- If the natural summary would otherwise be a single bare token or name, append a one-line bridge clause that identifies the missing relation and at least one retrieval target rather than ending with the bare token.
- Keep phrasing concise and factual; favor short lines, source-linked evidence excerpts, and explicit next-step instructions. Do not attempt to fetch pages yourself—only list retrieval targets/queries.

Example structure to follow in the `summary` field (use passage text and titles exactly):
Evidence found: ...
Ambiguities: ...
Bridge / missing fact: ...
Retrieval direction: ...

End of instructions.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Required output format and constraints:
- Produce exactly one field named `query`. Its value must be a single short, keyword-style search string (no full sentences, no explanations, no assertions, no user-facing clarifications).
- The query must be terse (preferably <= 12 words) and contain no clause punctuation or sentence grammar (no question marks, no leading "who/what is", no verbs forming assertions).
- Use only tokens that are visible in `question` and/or `summary_1`, except you may insert at most one minimal neutral connector token to target bridging evidence (allowed connector tokens include, but are not limited to: artist, album, director, who directed, biography, introduction, genre, regular season). Do not invent new entity names or facts.
- When the inputs present multiple entities or candidate items that must be linked (for example two titles, two names, or an anchor title plus a candidate), include both entity tokens from the inputs and a single neutral connector token between or adjacent to them to indicate the evidence bridge to retrieve (e.g., "EntityA EntityB director").
- If the question or summary includes structured tokens relevant to the bridge (years, league IDs, edition names), include those tokens plus a concise cue (for example year + league + "regular season") as part of the short query.
- You may perform light normalization of obvious misspellings in entity names or technical terms drawn from the visible inputs (fix clear typos), but do not invent alternative candidate entities or assert corrected identities beyond the token-level fix.
- If it is not possible to form a fully disambiguating query from visible tokens, still emit the best compact retrieval query possible using only visible tokens and an allowed connector; do not ask the user for clarification at this step. (Downstream retrieval will be attempted first; user clarification is allowed only after retrieval fails.)

Produce only the `query` field value (the single short search string) and nothing else.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single text field only):
- Output exactly one concise summary string (no extra fields, labels, or metadata). Keep the summary to 1–2 short sentences unless the evidence forces a single-token identification (see below).
- Primary goal: integrate the first-hop identity in `context` with facts found in `passages` so the final answer agent has the explicit, supported linkage it needs.

When the question requires linking the context entity to information in the passages and the passages supply the bridging fact:
- Produce a concise (1–2 sentence) integrated summary that (1) names the context entity (using the form present in `context`), (2) names the supporting entity/attribute found in `passages` (using the form present in the inputs), and (3) states the explicit relation or fact (membership, creator attribution, partnership, founding/establishment, award, etc.) exactly as supported by the inputs. Use only facts present in `context` and `passages` and avoid introducing any unsupported details.

Name and attribution formatting:
- If a canonical multi-token name (given + family name or full official name) appears in the inputs, use that full form in the summary; do not truncate to a surname or partial name.
- For questions about creator/author/credit, prefer the person-name attribution as given in the inputs over returning the title of a work or event.

Identification requests:
- If the question explicitly asks for a single identification (for example, asks "which X" or otherwise requests a named token or label), return exactly the canonical name(s) as presented in the passages. If multiple valid names are requested, return them comma-separated. Do not add explanatory clauses, relational fragments, or extra text in these cases—only the canonical name(s).

When evidence is missing or ambiguous:
- If the passages do not supply a clear bridging fact, state that succinctly (e.g., "No supporting evidence in the provided passages" or "Unclear from the provided sources"). Retain uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported facts.

Conciseness and fidelity constraints:
- Keep the summary short and factual (1–2 sentences). Do not expand into long biographies, exhaustive lists, or unrelated details.
- Do not assert facts that are not present in `context` and `passages`.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passage text, but short verbatim names or phrases necessary for fidelity are allowed.
- Treat the `context` field as the primary first-hop identity to be linked to `passages` when integrating evidence.

Produce the `summary` now based only on the supplied `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Use summary_1 and summary_2 as the sole evidential basis for the final answer; give greatest weight to summary_2 (the later, post-retrieval integrated evidence). Do not introduce facts from outside these summaries.

Output rules (apply in order):

1) If summary_1 and summary_2 clearly and unambiguously agree on the same concise answer, set the value of `answer` to exactly one minimal string and nothing else:
   - For a binary yes/no conclusion, emit the single lowercase token: yes or no.
   - For any other concise label, emit that label exactly as it appears in the summaries (trim leading/trailing whitespace and strip any surrounding punctuation or wrapper syntax such as surrounding parentheses, quotes, or JSON-like braces). Do not add words, sentences, punctuation, quotes, brackets, or any other surrounding text.

2) Preserve multi-token labels: if any visible summary contains a multi-token form of the answer (for example a full personal name, a multi-word company or place), output that full multi-token form verbatim. Only shorten a label if no multi-token form appears in either summary.

3) Company/developer preference: when the question requests a company or developer and the summaries show a subordinate studio together with an explicit parent/publisher label (e.g., in parentheses or with phrasing like "published by"), choose the parent/publisher name as the single minimal answer string. If no parent/publisher is explicitly indicated, choose the best-supported developer/studio name from the summaries.

4) If the summaries conflict or are ambiguous, do NOT apply the unanimous-agreement rule in (1). Instead, use summary_2 as the primary basis and emit the minimal concise string supported by summary_2, applying rules (2) and (3) as relevant. If summary_2 provides no concise supported answer, set `answer` to the minimal explicit token "unknown".

Formatting constraints (must always be followed):
- Produce exactly one field: `answer`.
- The value of `answer` must be a single minimal string (no surrounding text, punctuation, explanation, or structural wrappers).
- Do not output extra fields, labels, or markup.

Additional guidance:
- If summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge but summary_2 provides a resolved, supported conclusion, use the conclusion from summary_2.
- If a concise answer string can be drawn verbatim from the summaries, prefer that verbatim text over paraphrase.

End.

response.hop1_query: Susan May Pratt played the part of Mandella in a movie that came out in which year?
hop1_docs: Susan May Pratt | Susan May Pratt (born February 8, 1974) is an American actress.  She played Mandella in "10 Things I Hate About You", Alicia in "Drive Me Crazy", and Maureen Cummings in "Center Stage".

Seattle Sun (2002–05) | The Seattle Sun was a free, monthly neighborhood newspaper in Seattle, Washington.  Founded in 1997 by Clayton and Susan Brehme Park as the Jet City Maven, its first issue came out in March of that year.  The next issue came out in May, and beginning in July, the paper went monthly.  It originally covered only that part of Seattle north of N.E. 65th Street and east of Aurora Avenue N., but in September 1998 it was expanded to include all of the city north of the Lake Washington Ship Canal.

Lenora Mandella | Lenora Mandella (4 May 1931 - 12 August 2005) played shortstop and pitcher in the All-American Girls Professional Baseball League between 1949 and 1951.  She both batted and threw right-handed.  Her nickname was Smokey.  She measured in at 5 feet 7 inches and weighed 145 pounds.
Susan Pratt | Susan Pratt (born April 29, 1956) is an American actress.

Kyla Pratt | Kyla Alissa Pratt is an American actress.  In television, she is known for her roles in the popular U.S. children's television series "Barney & Friends" and the Disney Channel series "The Proud Family" (which lasted for 3 seasons) and "One on One" (which lasted for 5 seasons).  After playing the daughter of Eddie Murphy's character in the films "Dr. Dolittle" and "Dr. Dolittle 2", Pratt became the main character in the remake series of the franchise such as "Dr. Dolittle 3", "", and "".  Pratt has also been in the films "Fat Albert", "Hotel for Dogs", and "The Proud Family Movie".  She has also played in the series "Let's Stay Together".

Susan Peters (Nigerian actress) | Susan Peters (born May 30, 1980) is a multiple award-winning Nigerian actress with over 50 credits in Nollywood (Nigerian) movies.  She is a star on Nigerian TV, a successful model, interior designer and beauty salon owner.  Recently, she won the 2011 Afro Hollywood Best Actress (English) Award for her role in "Bursting Out", NAFCA Awards (Nollywood and African Film Critics Awards) North Carolina Nigerian Oscars: Best Actress in Supporting Role 2011 the BON (Best Of Nollywood) Best Supporting Actress Award 2011, and the Actress of the Year 2010 and Most Stylish Actress 2012 Awards from "City People Magazine".  In 2011, she made the December cover of the creatively acclaimed, arts and culture magazine, "Zen".  Said "Zen Magazine" editor, Arinze Nwokolo, "Susan Peters is a remarkable and an incredible talent in the movie industry!  Her dedication to her art is unmistakable..."

The Pedagogical Poem | The Pedagogical Poem (Russian: Педагогическая поэма , "Pedagogicheskaya poema " ), translated into English as "Road to Life", is a novel by Anton Makarenko, written in 1925-1935 and published in three parts in 1933-1935.  Part 1 of the novel came out in 1933 (in the Year XVII Almanac, part 3), Part 2 in 1935 (Year XVIII Almanac, book 5) and part 3 later the same year (Year XVIII Almanac, book 8).  As a separate edition, the book came out 1937, through Khudozhestvennaya Literatura.
response.summary_1:
Evidence found:
- Susan May Pratt — [1]: "Susan May Pratt (born February 8, 1974) is an American actress."

- 10 Things I Hate About You (title) — [1]: ""10 Things I Hate About You""
- Lenora Mandella (different person) — [3]: "Lenora Mandella (4 May 1931 - 12 August 2005)"
- Susan Pratt (different person) — [4]: "Susan Pratt (born April 29, 1956)"

Ambiguities:
- "Mandella": could refer to the film character (passage [1]) or the person "Lenora Mandella" (passage [3]).
- "Susan May Pratt" vs "Susan Pratt": two distinct names appear (passages [1] and [4]).

Bridge / missing fact:
- The release year of the film "10 Things I Hate About You" (the movie in which Susan May Pratt played Mandella) is not given in the provided passages.

Retrieval direction:
- Fetch page: "10 Things I Hate About You" (film) — check release date/year.
- Search query: "10 Things I Hate About You release date" or "10 Things I Hate About You (film) 1999" 
- If needed for disambiguation: fetch "Lenora Mandella" page and "Susan Pratt" page.
Final answer deferred pending retrieval of the targets above.
response.hop2_query: 10 Things I Hate About You release date
hop2_docs: 10 Things I Hate About You (TV series) | 10 Things I Hate About You is an American television sitcom broadcast on ABC Family beginning in 2009.  Developed by Carter Covington, the show is a half-hour, single camera series based on "Ten Things I Hate About You".  It premiered on Tuesday, July 7, 2009 at 8 pm and brought in 1.60 million viewers, a record for a 30-minute comedy debut on the ABC Family network.

10 Things I Hate About You | 10 Things I Hate About You is a 1999 American romantic comedy film directed by Gil Junger and starring Julia Stiles, Heath Ledger, Joseph Gordon-Levitt, and Larisa Oleynik.  The screenplay, written by Karen McCullah Lutz and Kirsten Smith, is a modernization of William Shakespeare's late-16th century comedy "The Taming of the Shrew", retold in a late-1990s American high school setting.  In the story, new student Cameron (Gordon-Levitt) is smitten with Bianca (Oleynik) and, in order to get around her father's strict rules on dating, attempts to get bad boy Patrick (Ledger) to date Bianca's ill-tempered sister, Kat (Stiles).  The film is titled after a poem written by Kat about her bittersweet romance with Patrick.  Much of the filming took place in the Seattle metropolitan area, with many scenes shot at Stadium High School in Tacoma.

10 Things I Hate About Life | 10 Things I Hate About Life is an cancelled/unfinished American romantic comedy film directed and written by Gil Junger.  The film stars Evan Rachel Wood, Thomas McDonell and Billy Campbell.

7 Things | "7 Things" is a song by American singer-songwriter and actress Miley Cyrus.  The song was co-written by Cyrus, Antonina Armato and Tim James, and produced by John Fields.  It was released on June 17, 2008 by Hollywood Records as the lead single from the first studio album released by Cyrus with no ties to her character Hannah Montana from the Disney Channel comedy series of the same name.  Also registered as "Seven Things I Hate About You", Cyrus developed the song during the Best of Both Worlds Tour while feeling numerous emotions for an ex-boyfriend, mainly anger.  The song's release brought allegations that it was about Nick Jonas of the Jonas Brothers, which Cyrus neither confirmed nor denied.  Musically, "7 Things" bears aspects of both country and pop punk, while the lyrics in the song's refrain list seven traits Cyrus hates about an ex-boyfriend.
Sexy Boy (Air song) | "Sexy Boy" is a single by French band Air and was featured in the film "10 Things I Hate About You" as well as the British television series "Queer as Folk".

Change (Miho Fukuhara song) | "Change" is the major-label debut single by Japanese soul singer Miho Fukuhara, telling of a girl who talks playfully about she loves a man, going as far as saying things like "I hate, hate, hate you!  Just kidding, I love you!" .  The single was released on April 16, 2008 to decent success regarding both sales and airplay, peaking at #14 on Oricon and #4 on Billboard Japan.  "Change" is still currently Fukuhara's best-selling single.

Dana Davis | Dana Davis (born October 4, 1978) is an American actress, known for playing Chastity Church on the ABC Family television series "10 Things I Hate About You" (2009–10) and Carmen Phillips on the TNT series "Franklin & Bash" (2011-2013).
hop2_titles: 10 Things I Hate About You (TV series)
response.summary_2: Susan May Pratt played Mandella in the film 10 Things I Hate About You, which was released in 1999.
